---
title: "Skeleton Fork Fern (Psilotum nudum)"
slug: "psilotum-nudum"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354593439/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Skeleton Fork Fern"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Polypodiopsida"
tax_order: "Psilotales"
tax_family: "Psilotaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Polypodiopsida", "Psilotales", "Psilotaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354593439/medium.jpg" alt="Skeleton Fork Fern" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/tiasilvasy">
tiasilvasy</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/200928643">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Polypodiopsida
-   **Order:** Psilotales
-   **Family:** Psilotaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=167517)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon species in Florida found in shaded, humid habitats such as hammocks, and tree bases.

## Natural history notes

A primitive, fern ally characterized by its green, leafless, branching stems that give it a skeletal appearance. In Florida, it is found in shaded, humid habitats such as hammocks, tree bases, and occasionally on rocks or old structures. It reproduces by spores rather than true seeds and often grows as an epiphyte or on organic debris in sheltered environments.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/167517-Psilotum-nudum)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/genus/1015)
